FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline data, 15,022 children aged 9-11, data from 21 U.S. sites, NIH-funded

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: mixed-effects regression models predicting children's screen time from family income and race

FT_IC2.evidence: Other-side variable (household income, race, covariates) is demographic-only. Step 1: no phone/SM-specific estimate found. Step 2: screen time composite includes TV, movies, computer, tablet, phone without separate breakout (Methods: "average weekdays...measured in hours"). Multi-device aggregated time fails IC2 without per-device isolation.

notes: Family income is purely demographic (socioeconomic indicator). Screen time is reported as undifferentiated composite without modality-specific breakdown.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline and 1-year follow-up data, 11,670 baseline participants (5,594 females, ages 9-10), 4,951 longitudinal subset

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) examining associations between sleep disturbance/total sleep time and mental health outcomes (depression, internalizing, externalizing)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper does not analyze smartphone or social media use as IV or DV. Primary IVs are sleep disturbance subscales (DIMS, DOES, SWTD) and total sleep time. DVs are depression/internalizing/externalizing from CBCL. Screen time included as covariate only (not as IV or DV in any qualifying analysis). No phone/SM variable on either side of any core relationship.

notes: Paper focuses on sleep-depression association. Screen time mentioned in context ('links between greater electronic device use and poor sleep') but not analyzed as a primary predictor. Does not meet scope (no qualifying smartphone/SM variable).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study Year 2 data (2018-2020), ages 10-13, 7,614 participants with high-quality structural MRI and complete covariates

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: generalized additive mixed models (gamma4 package) examining associations between social media use/addiction and brain morphology (cortical thickness, surface area, volume)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social media use ("hours per day" continuous measure, Methods: "Minutes and hours per day spent using social media") and Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ, Berg et al. 2012) are phone/SM-specific variables. Both paired with individual-trait-level outcome (neural: cortical thickness, surface area, volume). Directly meets Step 1 criteria; no need to proceed to Step 2.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: regression β coefficients for social media use with cortical thickness/surface area/volume across regions (Table 2, main text), standardized beta coefficients, p-values, and confidence intervals (e.g., superior frontal β=-3.1 ± 1.2, caudal anterior cingulate β=-1.3 ± 1.2). Vertexwise FEMA analysis with surface maps and heat maps provided.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric quantification: region-of-interest (ROI) mixed-effects models with β coefficients and 95% confidence intervals across 45+ brain regions; vertexwise analysis with Z-scores and statistical maps showing significant associations at Q < 0.05; effect sizes and p-values throughout

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (Year 2 snapshot data), not longitudinal. FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs. Additionally, social media is analyzed as IV (predictor of brain morphology), not as DV-only.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. Clear phone/SM-specific exposure (daily SM use hours, SMAQ addiction scale) paired with neural individual-trait outcomes (brain morphology). Cross-sectional design precludes FT-EC7.
